original_hypothesis.title: Unbounded ownedCurvesTokenSubjects Array Growth Causes transferAllCurvesTokens Gas DoS
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ownedCurvesTokenSubjects array never removes entries even when balance reaches 0 after selling. An attacker can inflate a victim's array by transferring 1 token from many different subjects to the victim's address. Each transfer calls _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject which adds the subject (with O(n) duplicate check). When the victim calls transferAllCurvesTokens, the unbounded iteration causes out-of-gas.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject (novel) iterates the entire array to check for duplicates, and the array never shrinks. transferAllCurvesTokens (novel) iterates the entire array to transfer all tokens. Both become gas-prohibitive as the array grows.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject iterates full array for duplicate check, never removes entries; array grows monotonically

preconditions: Many curve subjects exist in the protocol
Attacker can buy tokens in multiple subjects and transfer 1 to victim
Victim relies on transferAllCurvesTokens for migration

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject: O(n) duplicate check per addition; transferAllCurvesTokens: O(n) iteration with _transfer per entry
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Transfer 1 token from 1000 different subjects to victim → array length 1000 → gas cost ~30M+ for transferAllCurvesTokens
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack - victim cannot use transferAllCurvesTokens for account migration

economic_questions: What is the cost to buy minimum tokens in N subjects?
At what array size does transferAllCurvesTokens exceed block gas limit?
Can victim still transfer individual tokens as fallback?
validation_requirements: Estimate gas per iteration in transferAllCurvesTokens
Calculate block gas limit threshold for array size
Verify entries are never removed on sell/transfer-away

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost to buy 1 token in each of N subjects (bonding curve cost at supply+1)

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: ETH for multiple minimum token purchases

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Convenience loss - individual transfers still work

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Gas cost per array iteration, block gas limit
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Simulate array growth to DoS threshold

validation_result.mechanism: Unbounded array growth with no cleanup
validation_result.impact_summary: An attacker can cheaply inflate any victim's ownedCurvesTokenSubjects array by creating many curves (first token is free) and transferring 1 token to the victim, permanently rendering transferAllCurvesTokens unusable due to out-of-gas.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The ownedCurvesTokenSubjects array for each address grows monotonically — entries are added by _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject but never removed, even when a user's balance for that subject drops to zero. An attacker can exploit this by: (1) creating curves for addresses they control (first token is free since getPrice(0,1) returns 0), (2) transferring 1 token from each curve to the victim via transferCurvesToken. Each transfer calls _transfer which calls _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject(to, curvesTokenSubject), appending the new subject to the victim's array. When the victim later calls transferAllCurvesTokens, it iterates the entire array, and for each entry with balance > 0, it calls _transfer which in turn calls _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject on the recipient's array with an O(n) duplicate scan. At sufficient array sizes (hundreds to low thousands of entries), this exceeds the block gas limit.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code clearly shows: (1) _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject performs O(n) linear scan and only appends, never removes. (2) transferAllCurvesTokens iterates the full array including zero-balance stale entries. (3) _transfer unconditionally calls _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject for the recipient. (4) No function anywhere removes entries from ownedCurvesTokenSubjects. (5) getPrice(0,1) returns 0, making first-token creation free for the subject address.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: O(n) duplicate check with no removal mechanism. Array grows monotonically for each unique subject transferred to the owner.
Unbounded iteration over the full array including zero-balance stale entries. Each _transfer call also triggers _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject on the recipient with O(n) scan, compounding gas cost.
Every transfer to a new address adds the subject to the recipient's ownedCurvesTokenSubjects array via _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject, enabling an attacker to inflate any victim's array.
getPrice(0, 1) returns 0, making the first token of any curve free for the subject. This means an attacker can create unlimited curves at addresses they control for zero ETH cost (only gas).

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker creates address_N as a new EOA, calls buyCurvesToken(address_N, 1) from address_N — supply=0 so msg.sender==subject check passes, price=0 so free
2. Attacker calls transferCurvesToken(address_N, victim, 1) from address_N — this calls _transfer(address_N, address_N, victim, 1)
3. _transfer calls _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject(victim, address_N) — adds address_N to victim's ownedCurvesTokenSubjects array
4. Repeat steps 1-3 for N different attacker-controlled addresses
5. Victim's ownedCurvesTokenSubjects array now has N entries
6. Victim calls transferAllCurvesTokens(newAddress) — iterates all N entries, each with balance check and potential _transfer + _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject on recipient
7. At sufficiently large N, gas exceeds block gas limit → transaction reverts
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The attacker exploits the free first-token creation (getPrice(0,1)=0) combined with transferCurvesToken to inflate the victim's ownedCurvesTokenSubjects array. The array in _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject (Curves.sol lines 264-271) never removes entries and performs O(n) duplicate checks. transferAllCurvesTokens (Curves.sol lines 241-249) iterates the full array. With N subjects, the gas cost for transferAllCurvesTokens is approximately O(N) for the outer loop times O(N) for _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject on the recipient = O(N^2) total, quickly exceeding block gas limits.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that affect this vulnerability. The defect is inherent in the contract logic.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is highly realistic and cheap. The attacker generates many EOA addresses, calls buyCurvesToken from each (first token is free, only gas cost), then transfers 1 token to the victim. Each unique subject adds an entry to the victim's ownedCurvesTokenSubjects. The array never shrinks. At ~500-1000 entries, transferAllCurvesTokens would likely exceed the 30M block gas limit due to the O(N) outer loop with nested O(N) _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject calls on the recipient. The victim can still use transferCurvesToken individually, but transferAllCurvesTokens (the migration function) is permanently bricked.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. The transferAllCurvesTokens function becomes permanently unusable for targeted accounts. This is a griefing/DoS attack with no direct fund theft. The victim retains the ability to transfer tokens individually via transferCurvesToken, but loses the batch migration functionality. The attack cost is very low (only gas for creating curves and transferring tokens, no ETH cost for token purchases).
recommended_mitigations: Remove entries from ownedCurvesTokenSubjects when a user's balance for that subject reaches 0 (e.g., swap-and-pop pattern in sellCurvesToken and _transfer)
Add a maximum length limit to ownedCurvesTokenSubjects per address
Use a mapping(address => mapping(address => bool)) to track subject ownership instead of an unbounded array, with a separate paginated enumeration mechanism
Add pagination support to transferAllCurvesTokens (e.g., startIndex, endIndex parameters)
Require minimum token amount > 0 for transfers to prevent 1-token griefing
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Curves with factory and fee splitter. 2. Create 1000 different EOA addresses. 3. From each EOA, call buyCurvesToken(eoaAddress, 1) with 0 ETH (first token is free). 4. From each EOA, call transferCurvesToken(eoaAddress, victim, 1). 5. Verify victim's ownedCurvesTokenSubjects length is 1000. 6. Call transferAllCurvesTokens from victim — measure gas, expect out-of-gas at block limit.
uncertainties: Exact array length threshold for block gas limit DoS depends on the chain's gas limit and EVM gas costs
The victim can still use individual transferCurvesToken calls as a workaround, limiting practical impact to convenience
additional_testing_needed: Measure exact gas cost per iteration in transferAllCurvesTokens including the nested _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject call
Determine the threshold array size that causes out-of-gas at 30M block gas limit
Calculate total attacker gas cost to reach the DoS threshold
Test whether selling/transferring away all tokens of a subject leaves the stale entry (confirmed by code review but should be tested)
